draft.introduction: Thomas More's Utopia (1516) describes an imaginary island society organized around reason, communal property, and religious tolerance. The word "utopia" (from Greek ou-topos, "no place") entered every European language as the name for an ideal society.

translation.text: The island of Utopia is two hundred miles across in the middle part, where it is widest. No one there thinks of any property as his own. They change houses every ten years, casting lots for the exchange. They regard gold and silver as substances of no more worth than iron; with gold they make chamber pots and chains for slaves, so that these metals may be held in universal contempt. Pearls and diamonds they give to children, who take pride in them for a few years but then, as they grow up, cast them aside as childish things—just as our children outgrow their puppets.

translator_notes.note: The genius of Utopia lies in its irresolvable ambiguity: More presents the Utopian system with apparent admiration while embedding signals that undercut it. The name means "no place," and the narrator is called Hythlodaeus ("speaker of nonsense"). More himself died for his Catholic convictions, which the Utopians' religious tolerance would have undermined. The work inaugurated the entire genre of utopian literature.
